(**I-86**) is an [interstate highway](/wiki/Interstate_Highway "Interstate Highway") that extends for 254.02 miles (408.81 km) through northwestern [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") and the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier") region of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), in the United States. The highway has two segments: the longer of the two begins at an interchange with [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90 "Interstate 90") east of [Erie, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Erie,_Pennsylvania "Erie, Pennsylvania"), and ends at [New York State Route 26](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_26 "New York State Route 26") (NY 26) in [Vestal, New York](/wiki/Vestal,_New_York "Vestal, New York"), in [Broome County](/wiki/Broome_County,_New_York "Broome County, New York"), while the second extends from [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81 "Interstate 81") east of [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York") to [NY 79](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_79 "New York State Route 79") in [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_(village),_New_York "Windsor (village), New York"). When projects to upgrade the existing [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17") to [Interstate Highway standards](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_standards "Interstate Highway standards") are completed, I-86 will extend from I-90 near Erie to the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") ([I-87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)")) in [Woodbury](/wiki/Woodbury,_Orange_County,_New_York "Woodbury, Orange County, New York"). The portion in Erie County, Pennsylvania, is known as the **Hopkins-Bowser Highway** and is signed as such at each end. In New York, the current and future alignment of I-86 is known as the **Southern Tier Expressway** west of I-81 in Binghamton and the **Quickway** east of I-81.

of the [Appalachian Development Highway System](/wiki/Appalachian_Development_Highway_System "Appalachian Development Highway System"). I-86 connects to [US Route 219](/wiki/U.S._Route_219 "U.S. Route 219") (US 219) in [Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca_(city),_New_York "Salamanca (city), New York"), [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") near [Avoca](/wiki/Avoca_(village),_New_York "Avoca (village), New York"), and [I-99](/wiki/Interstate_99 "Interstate 99")/[US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15 "U.S. Route 15") just west of [Corning](/wiki/Corning_(city),_New_York "Corning (city), New York").

(**PA 17**) and to the westernmost 177 miles (285 km) of NY 17. It has been extended eastward as more sections of the existing NY 17 freeway have been upgraded to Interstate Highway standards, first to [NY 14](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14 "New York State Route 14") in [Horseheads](/wiki/Horseheads_(village),_New_York "Horseheads (village), New York") in 2004, to NY 352 in Elmira in 2008, to the Chemung–Tioga county line in 2013, and to exit 67 ([NY 26](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_26 "New York State Route 26")) in [Vestal](/wiki/Vestal,_New_York "Vestal, New York") in 2024.[[4]](#cite_note-4). The segment of NY 17 between I-81 and NY 79 was designated as part of I-86 in 2006, but this segment currently remains discontinuous with the rest of I-86 while work is being done in the Binghamton area to bring NY 17 up to Interstate standards.

**Interstate 86** (**I-86**) is an [interstate highway](/wiki/Interstate_Highway "Interstate Highway") that extends for 254.02 miles (408.81 km) through northwestern [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") and the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier") region of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), in the United States. The highway has two segments: the longer of the two begins at an interchange with [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90 "Interstate 90") east of [Erie, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Erie,_Pennsylvania "Erie, Pennsylvania"), and ends at [New York State Route 26](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_26 "New York State Route 26") (NY 26) in [Vestal, New York](/wiki/Vestal,_New_York "Vestal, New York"), in [Broome County](/wiki/Broome_County,_New_York "Broome County, New York"), while the second extends from [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81 "Interstate 81") east of [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York") to [NY 79](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_79 "New York State Route 79") in [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_(village),_New_York "Windsor (village), New York"). When projects to upgrade the existing [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17") to [Interstate Highway standards](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_standards "Interstate Highway standards") are completed, I-86 will extend from I-90 near Erie to the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") ([I-87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)")) in [Woodbury](/wiki/Woodbury,_Orange_County,_New_York "Woodbury, Orange County, New York"). The portion in Erie County, Pennsylvania, is known as the **Hopkins-Bowser Highway** and is signed as such at each end. In New York, the current and future alignment of I-86 is known as the **Southern Tier Expressway** west of I-81 in Binghamton and the **Quickway** east of I-81.

I-86 travels 7.00 miles (11.27 km) in Pennsylvania and 247.02 miles (397.54 km) in New York. Except for a section of about 1.5 miles (2.4 km) that dips into Pennsylvania at exit 60 near the New York village of [Waverly](/wiki/Waverly,_Tioga_County,_New_York "Waverly, Tioga County, New York"), the Pennsylvania borough of [South Waverly](/wiki/South_Waverly,_Pennsylvania "South Waverly, Pennsylvania"), and the section passing through [Greenfield Township](/wiki/Greenfield_Township,_Erie_County,_Pennsylvania "Greenfield Township, Erie County, Pennsylvania") from [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90 "Interstate 90") to the Pennsylvania–New York state line, the rest of I-86 will be in New York. The section of NY 17 through South Waverly is maintained by the [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") (NYSDOT), however. The Southern Tier Expressway section of I-86 and NY 17 comprises **Corridor T** of the [Appalachian Development Highway System](/wiki/Appalachian_Development_Highway_System "Appalachian Development Highway System"). I-86 connects to [US Route 219](/wiki/U.S._Route_219 "U.S. Route 219") (US 219) in [Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca_(city),_New_York "Salamanca (city), New York"), [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") near [Avoca](/wiki/Avoca_(village),_New_York "Avoca (village), New York"), and [I-99](/wiki/Interstate_99 "Interstate 99")/[US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15 "U.S. Route 15") just west of [Corning](/wiki/Corning_(city),_New_York "Corning (city), New York").

Most of the Quickway and the Southern Tier Expressway was built in stages from the 1950s to the 1980s. The I-86 designation was assigned on December 3, 1999, to the entirety of since-decommissioned **Pennsylvania Route 17** (**PA 17**) and to the westernmost 177 miles (285 km) of NY 17. It has been extended eastward as more sections of the existing NY 17 freeway have been upgraded to Interstate Highway standards, first to [NY 14](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14 "New York State Route 14") in [Horseheads](/wiki/Horseheads_(village),_New_York "Horseheads (village), New York") in 2004, to NY 352 in Elmira in 2008, to the Chemung–Tioga county line in 2013, and to exit 67 ([NY 26](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_26 "New York State Route 26")) in [Vestal](/wiki/Vestal,_New_York "Vestal, New York") in 2024.[[4]](#cite_note-4). The segment of NY 17 between I-81 and NY 79 was designated as part of I-86 in 2006, but this segment currently remains discontinuous with the rest of I-86 while work is being done in the Binghamton area to bring NY 17 up to Interstate standards.

### Pennsylvania to Olean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pennsylvania to Olean")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/2022-06-05_09_09_22_View_west_along_Interstate_86_west_of_Exit_3_in_Greenfield_Township%2C_Erie_County%2C_Pennsylvania.jpg/250px-2022-06-05_09_09_22_View_west_along_Interstate_86_west_of_Exit_3_in_Greenfield_Township%2C_Erie_County%2C_Pennsylvania.jpg)](/wiki/File:2022-06-05_09_09_22_View_west_along_Interstate_86_west_of_Exit_3_in_Greenfield_Township,_Erie_County,_Pennsylvania.jpg)

I-86 westbound past PA 89 in Greenfield Township

I-86 begins at an interchange with [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90 "Interstate 90") in a relatively flat area of northwestern [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). It heads to the southeast, meeting [PA 89](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_89 "Pennsylvania Route 89") at exit 3 before curving to the east and crossing into [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), where it becomes [concurrent](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17"). The freeway heads generally eastward across southwest [Chautauqua County](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York"), serving the [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)") of [Findley Lake](/wiki/Findley_Lake,_New_York "Findley Lake, New York") and the village of [Sherman](/wiki/Sherman_(village),_New_York "Sherman (village), New York") via [NY 426](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_426 "New York State Route 426") and [NY 76](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_76 "New York State Route 76"), respectively, as it proceeds toward [Chautauqua Lake](/wiki/Chautauqua_Lake "Chautauqua Lake").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Entering_Pennsylvania_on_I-86_west.jpg/250px-Entering_Pennsylvania_on_I-86_west.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entering_Pennsylvania_on_I-86_west.jpg)

Entering Pennsylvania on I-86 westbound in Erie County

After crossing Chautauqua Lake, I-86 merges into an older section of freeway at exit 10 near [Bemus Point](/wiki/Bemus_Point,_New_York "Bemus Point, New York"); this freeway is now [NY 954J](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_954J "New York State Route 954J") northwest of the newer extension. NY 954J runs into [NY 430](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_430 "New York State Route 430"), which (along with [NY 394](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_394 "New York State Route 394")) carried NY 17 to [Westfield](/wiki/Westfield_(village),_New_York "Westfield (village), New York") before the 1980s extension. From Bemus Point to [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York") (exit 12), I-86 parallels the old NY 17 (now NY 430) along the northeast shore of Chautauqua Lake. The [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") extension to [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") (built as the [Atlantic and Great Western Railroad](/wiki/Atlantic_and_Great_Western_Railroad "Atlantic and Great Western Railroad")) comes into Jamestown from the southwest and parallels I-86 to its junction with the Erie's original main line to [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk,_New_York "Dunkirk, New York") at [Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca_(city),_New_York "Salamanca (city), New York").

From Jamestown to Salamanca, the old NY 17 (now mostly NY 394), the new I-86 and the railroad run generally parallel through river valleys. The transportation routes run along the [Chadakoin River](/wiki/Chadakoin_River "Chadakoin River"), [Conewango Creek](/wiki/Conewango_Creek "Conewango Creek") and Little Conewango Creek to [Steamburg](/wiki/Steamburg,_New_York "Steamburg, New York") (exit 17), cutting east to the [Allegheny River](/wiki/Allegheny_River "Allegheny River") at [Coldspring](/wiki/Coldspring,_New_York "Coldspring, New York") there. The valley of the Allegheny takes the routes to Salamanca (exit 20), where the railroads merged, and beyond to [Olean](/wiki/Olean,_New_York "Olean, New York") (exits 25 and 26). From Salamanca to Olean, the old NY 17 is now [NY 417](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_417 "New York State Route 417"). At Olean, the Allegheny River and NY 417 (old NY 17) continue southeast, while I-86 and the Erie Railroad head northeast. NY 417 does not return to I-86 until exit 44 near [Painted Post](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York"), and the Erie switches between the two alignments several times.

### Olean to Elmira

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Olean to Elmira")]

I-86 and the old Erie line (now part of the [Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Western_New_York_and_Pennsylvania_Railroad_(2001) "Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad (2001)")) run northeast along the valleys of Olean Creek and [Oil Creek](/wiki/Oil_Creek_(Allegheny_River) "Oil Creek (Allegheny River)") to [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba_(village),_New_York "Cuba (village), New York") (exit 28). From Cuba to [Friendship](/wiki/Friendship,_New_York "Friendship, New York") (exit 29), they run through a valley and over a summit, then following Van Campen Creek northeast to [Belvidere](/wiki/Belvidere,_New_York "Belvidere, New York") (exit 30). At Belvidere, the Erie turns southeast to meet NY 417 at [Wellsville](/wiki/Wellsville_(village),_New_York "Wellsville (village), New York"), but I-86 continues northeast through the valleys of the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") and Angelica Creek to [Angelica](/wiki/Angelica_(village),_New_York "Angelica (village), New York") (exit 31), and then east along Angelica Creek, over a summit that is the highest point on the Interstate, and along Karr Valley Creek to [Almond](/wiki/Almond_(village),_New_York "Almond (village), New York") (exit 33). This summit, at 2,110 feet (640 m) above [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level"), is the highest point along I-86, located between exits 32 ([West Almond](/wiki/West_Almond,_New_York "West Almond, New York")) and 33 and marked with a sign.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Highest_elevation_on_I-86.jpg/250px-Highest_elevation_on_I-86.jpg)](/wiki/File:Highest_elevation_on_I-86.jpg)

Signage denoting the highest point on I-86 in Almond

At Almond, I-86 rejoins the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad"), passing through Canacadea Creek valley about halfway to [Hornellsville](/wiki/Hornellsville,_New_York "Hornellsville, New York"). However, where the railroad turns southeast to Hornellsville, I-86 continues northeast across a summit and into the wide [Canisteo River](/wiki/Canisteo_River "Canisteo River") valley (exit 34). It leaves the valley along Carrington Creek but quickly turns east across a summit to follow Big Creek and cross another summit to [Howard](/wiki/Howard,_New_York "Howard, New York") (exit 35). I-86 runs alongside Goff Creek from Howard to the wide [Cohocton River](/wiki/Cohocton_River "Cohocton River") valley, where it meets the south end of [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") (exit 36) near Avoca and turns southeast through that valley, parallel to the Erie's [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York")–[Painted Post](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York") line ([Buffalo, New York](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), and [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad")).

I-86, [NY 415](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_415 "New York State Route 415") (old [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15 "U.S. Route 15")) and the Erie branch all run southeast along the Cohocton River past [Bath](/wiki/Bath_(village),_New_York "Bath (village), New York") (exit 38) to [Painted Post](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York") (exit 44), now the north end of [I-99](/wiki/Interstate_99 "Interstate 99") and US 15. NY 417 (old NY 17) also ends at exit 44, while NY 415 continues east into Corning (exits 45–46). From Painted Post through Corning to [Big Flats](/wiki/Big_Flats_(CDP),_New_York "Big Flats (CDP), New York") (exit 49), I-86, [NY 352](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_352 "New York State Route 352") (old NY 17) and the Erie Railroad run through the [Chemung River](/wiki/Chemung_River "Chemung River") valley. NY 352 begins at exit 45, west of downtown Corning, and is a recently bypassed four-lane road through Corning. East of [East Corning](/wiki/East_Corning,_New_York "East Corning, New York") (exit 48), the freeway was built as an on-the-spot upgrade of the old NY 17.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/I86-NY17_west_in_Chemung_County.jpg/250px-I86-NY17_west_in_Chemung_County.jpg)](/wiki/File:I86-NY17_west_in_Chemung_County.jpg)

I-86 westbound in Chemung County

At Big Flats, the Chemung River (and NY 352) turns southeast to downtown [Elmira](/wiki/Elmira,_New_York "Elmira, New York"), while I-86 and the Erie continue east-northeast alongside Singsing Creek to the vicinity of [Elmira Corning Regional Airport](/wiki/Elmira_Corning_Regional_Airport "Elmira Corning Regional Airport"). The highway continues into [Horseheads](/wiki/Horseheads_(village),_New_York "Horseheads (village), New York"), where it becomes an elevated highway through the use of a large arrangement of embankments and bridges. It connects to [NY 14](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14 "New York State Route 14") and [NY 13](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_13 "New York State Route 13") via exits 52 and 54, respectively, before turning south to follow Newtown Creek into Elmira. Just east of the city's downtown district, I-86 meets [NY 352](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_352 "New York State Route 352") (exit 56), then continues to the Chemung–Tioga county line.

### Elmira to Vestal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Elmira to Vestal")]

From NY 352 onwards, I-86 straddles the Chemung River past Elmira towards [Waverly](/wiki/Waverly,_Tioga_County,_New_York "Waverly, Tioga County, New York"), where it briefly dips back into Pennsylvania for a few yards and intersects [U.S. Route 220](/wiki/U.S._Route_220 "U.S. Route 220") before turning back into New York, now straddling the [Susquehanna River](/wiki/Susquehanna_River "Susquehanna River"). The freeway passes by the [Tioga Downs](/wiki/Tioga_Downs "Tioga Downs") racetrack and casino in [Nichols](/wiki/Nichols,_New_York "Nichols, New York") before an interchange with [NY 96](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_96 "New York State Route 96") and [NY 434](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_434 "New York State Route 434") in [Owego](/wiki/Owego,_New_York "Owego, New York").

I-86 and NY 17 continue following the Susquehanna into [Broome County](/wiki/Broome_County,_New_York "Broome County, New York") with NY 434, the former NY 17, running parallel to the freeway. I-86 and NY 17 then intersects with [NY 26](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_26 "New York State Route 26") at a clover interchange in Vestal, with NY 17 continuing along the freeway towards [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York"). The I-86 designation ends here for now; however, a 9.9-mile (15.9 km) section of NY 17 just east of Binghamton is also designated as I-86, creating a temporary gap in the designation. This segment runs from [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81 "Interstate 81") at exit 75 in [Kirkwood](/wiki/Kirkwood,_New_York "Kirkwood, New York") to [NY 79](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_79 "New York State Route 79") at exit 79 in [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_(village),_New_York "Windsor (village), New York").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins and the Quickway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Origins and the Quickway")]

See also: [New York State Route 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17")

The first long-distance route through the modern I-86 corridor was [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17"), which extended from [Westfield](/wiki/Westfield_(village),_New_York "Westfield (village), New York") to [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") via [Harriman](/wiki/Harriman,_New_York "Harriman, New York") when it was assigned in 1924.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Much of NY 17 followed a routing parallel or identical to that of the modern Southern Tier Expressway and Quickway; however, it followed a more northerly routing between Westfield and [Bemus Point](/wiki/Bemus_Point,_New_York "Bemus Point, New York") (via modern [NY 394](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_394 "New York State Route 394") and [NY 430](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_430 "New York State Route 430")) and a more southerly track from [Belvidere](/wiki/Belvidere,_New_York "Belvidere, New York") to [Corning](/wiki/Corning_(city),_New_York "Corning (city), New York") (via what is now [NY 19](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_19 "New York State Route 19") and [NY 417](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_417 "New York State Route 417")).[[8]](#cite_note-8) NY 17 was realigned as part of the [1930 state highway renumbering](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)") to travel directly from [Olean](/wiki/Olean,_New_York "Olean, New York") to [Wellsville](/wiki/Wellsville_(village),_New_York "Wellsville (village), New York") on modern NY 417, located well to the south of today's Southern Tier Expressway.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/SR_17_NY.jpg/250px-SR_17_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:SR_17_NY.jpg)

NY 17 (future I-86) at Liberty

By the late 1940s, the portion of NY 17 through the [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains") and [Orange](/wiki/Orange_County,_New_York "Orange County, New York") and [Rockland](/wiki/Rockland_County,_New_York "Rockland County, New York") counties had become prone to massive traffic jams due to both its winding and narrow composition and congestion in the villages and [hamlets](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)") along the highway. As a result, the state of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") began making plans to construct an expressway leading from the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") at Harriman to the Catskills.[[10]](#cite_note-nyt-1958-10) Construction of the NY 17 freeway began in 1947 in the Hudson Valley town of [Wallkill](/wiki/Wallkill,_Orange_County,_New_York "Wallkill, Orange County, New York").[[11]](#cite_note-nbi-11) The first section of the new freeway, a bypass of [Middletown](/wiki/Middletown,_Orange_County,_New_York "Middletown, Orange County, New York") between [Fair Oaks](/wiki/Fair_Oaks,_New_York "Fair Oaks, New York") (exit 118A, since removed) and [Goshen](/wiki/Goshen_(village),_New_York "Goshen (village), New York") (exit 123), opened to traffic in July 1951 as a realignment of NY 17. In 1954, several severe accidents occurred along parts of the surface NY 17, compelling the state to make constructing the freeway, dubbed the "Quickway", a higher priority.[[10]](#cite_note-nyt-1958-10)

The road was extended east first, reaching [Chester](/wiki/Chester_(village),_New_York "Chester (village), New York") (exit 127) in October 1954 and the Thruway near Harriman in August 1955.[[10]](#cite_note-nyt-1958-10) To the west, a section of the highway through [Sullivan](/wiki/Sullivan_County,_New_York "Sullivan County, New York") and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_County,_New_York "Delaware County, New York") counties was built over the [right-of-way](/wiki/Right-of-way_(transportation) "Right-of-way (transportation)") of the defunct [New York, Ontario and Western Railway](/wiki/New_York,_Ontario_and_Western_Railway "New York, Ontario and Western Railway").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Most of the Sullivan County section of the Quickway was completed during the 1950s, with the first section within the county—between Rock Hill (exit 108) and [Wurtsboro](/wiki/Wurtsboro,_New_York "Wurtsboro, New York") (exit 114)—opening in December 1956. A second section, from Ferndale (exit 101) to north of [Liberty](/wiki/Liberty_(village),_New_York "Liberty (village), New York") (exit 98), was completed in July 1958. The gap between Wurtsboro and Fair Oaks in [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_New_York "Orange County, New York") was filled on October 23, 1958, while the section between Ferndale and Rock Hill was completed in two stages. The section east of modern exit 104 in [Monticello](/wiki/Monticello,_New_York "Monticello, New York") was opened in July 1959; the part north of that point opened in December 1960.[[10]](#cite_note-nyt-1958-10)

By 1969, with the assistance of federal funding from the [Appalachian Regional Commission](/wiki/Appalachian_Regional_Commission "Appalachian Regional Commission") procured by New York's US Senator [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the 130-mile (210 km) route provided nonstop access between Harriman and [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York"), and by extension [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") to Binghamton. It connected the New York State Thruway ([I-87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)")) to [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81 "Interstate 81"). Despite flaws in the highway's design—it included a grade-level railroad crossing near [Fair Oaks](/wiki/Wallkill,_Orange_County,_New_York "Wallkill, Orange County, New York") (since removed) and two stretches with intersections and driveway access—the Quickway succeeded in easing travel through southern New York, cutting the driving time in half and the accident rate by 70 percent.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Southern Tier Expressway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Southern Tier Expressway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/State_border_sign_on_NY_17.jpg/250px-State_border_sign_on_NY_17.jpg)](/wiki/File:State_border_sign_on_NY_17.jpg)

Sign along eastbound NY 17 (future I-86) marking return to New York after its brief foray into Pennsylvania

In February 1953, New York Governor [Thomas E. Dewey](/wiki/Thomas_E._Dewey "Thomas E. Dewey") proposed constructing four superhighways across the state to supplement the New York State Thruway. One of the four proposed [limited-access highways](/wiki/Limited-access_highway "Limited-access highway") would cut across the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier"), linking [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90 "Interstate 90") in the west to Binghamton in the east.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The first segments of what became known as the Southern Tier Expressway, a westward continuation of the Quickway, were completed in the mid-1960s. Four sections were opened to traffic at this time: [Kennedy](/wiki/Kennedy,_New_York "Kennedy, New York") (exit 14) to [Randolph](/wiki/Randolph_(CDP),_New_York "Randolph (CDP), New York") (exit 16), [Coldspring](/wiki/Coldspring,_New_York "Coldspring, New York") (exit 17) to western [Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca_(city),_New_York "Salamanca (city), New York") (exit 20), [Campbell](/wiki/Campbell,_New_York "Campbell, New York") (exit 41) to [Corning](/wiki/Corning_(city),_New_York "Corning (city), New York") (exit 45), and [East Corning](/wiki/East_Corning,_New_York "East Corning, New York") (exit 48) to [Lowman](/wiki/Lowman,_New_York "Lowman, New York") (exit 57), parts of which were built as a surface highway.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) The Coldspring to Salamanca section was built out of necessity: in 1967, the first [stress test](/wiki/Stress_testing "Stress testing") of the [Kinzua Dam](/wiki/Kinzua_Dam "Kinzua Dam") had submerged part of the original NY 17 into the [Allegheny Reservoir](/wiki/Allegheny_Reservoir "Allegheny Reservoir") and made it impassable. Construction of the new highway destroyed most of the town of [Red House](/wiki/Red_House,_New_York "Red House, New York").[[16]](#cite_note-loe2004-16)[[17]](#cite_note-wgrz2014-17)

A fifth section, from [Owego](/wiki/Owego_(village),_New_York "Owego (village), New York") (exit 65) to [Johnson City](/wiki/Johnson_City,_New_York "Johnson City, New York") (exit 69), opened in early 1969.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The portion of the expressway between [Nichols](/wiki/Nichols_(village),_New_York "Nichols (village), New York") (exit 62) and Owego was opened to traffic on October 3, 1969.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Southern_Tier_Expressway.svg/120px-Southern_Tier_Expressway.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Southern_Tier_Expressway.svg)

Route marker used along the Southern Tier Expressway

Four more segments of the Southern Tier Expressway were completed over the course of the next three years. By 1972, the gaps between Randolph and Coldspring and from Johnson City to I-81 in Binghamton were filled while the expressway was extended west from Kennedy to [Falconer](/wiki/Falconer,_New_York "Falconer, New York") (exit 13) and east from Lowman to [Waverly](/wiki/Waverly,_Tioga_County,_New_York "Waverly, Tioga County, New York") (exit 60).[[20]](#cite_note-20) The missing link between Waverly and Nichols was completed by 1974.[[21]](#cite_note-1974map-21) The portion of the freeway in and around Waverly was originally planned to be built on the right-of-way of the [Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad](/wiki/Delaware,_Lackawanna_and_Western_Railroad "Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad") through southern Waverly; however, the plan was scrapped in favor of a more southerly alignment that passed through the borough of [South Waverly, Pennsylvania](/wiki/South_Waverly,_Pennsylvania "South Waverly, Pennsylvania"). The realignment saved $2 million (equivalent to $11.8 million in 2024[[22]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-22)) in construction costs and spared a handful of industries in the highway's proposed path. Both state legislatures approved the realignment in 1966 after New York agreed to maintain the section of the freeway in Pennsylvania. As part of an agreement made between the two states, Pennsylvania acquired the necessary right-of-way and easements for the freeway at the expense of New York.[[23]](#cite_note-inflation-US-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Three other segments of the freeway were completed by 1974. Two of the three—from [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York") (exit 11) and Falconer and between [Almond](/wiki/Almond_(village),_New_York "Almond (village), New York") (exit 33) and [Campbell](/wiki/Campbell_(CDP),_New_York "Campbell (CDP), New York")—were extensions of preexisting sections while the third, connecting [Allegany](/wiki/Allegany_(village),_New_York "Allegany (village), New York") (exit 24) to [Hinsdale](/wiki/Hinsdale,_New_York "Hinsdale, New York") (exit 27), was isolated from the other portions of the highway.[[21]](#cite_note-1974map-21) This was only temporary, however, as the freeway was completed between Hinsdale and Belvidere (exit 30) by January 1975 and opened to traffic from Belvidere to Almond on January 30, 1975.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The Southern Tier Expressway was extended westward to [Bemus Point](/wiki/Bemus_Point,_New_York "Bemus Point, New York") by 1977, initially utilizing what is now [NY 954J](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_954J "New York State Route 954J").[[26]](#cite_note-26) In the early 1980s, work began on a westward extension to the vicinity of [Erie, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Erie,_Pennsylvania "Erie, Pennsylvania"). The [Findley Lake](/wiki/Findley_Lake,_New_York "Findley Lake, New York")–Bemus Point (exits 4–10) segment was completed by 1985[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-1985map-28) while the portion from I-90 east of Erie to Findley Lake was opened by 1989. From I-90 to exit 8, the freeway was initially built as a [super two](/wiki/Super_two "Super two") highway, with both directions utilizing what are now the eastbound lanes.[[29]](#cite_note-1989map-29) The westbound lanes were built at a cost of $34 million (equivalent to $61.5 million in 2024[[22]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-22)) and opened to traffic on October 2, 1997.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[23]](#cite_note-inflation-US-23)

#### Salamanca and Corning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Salamanca and Corning")]

Construction of the freeway between exits 20 and 24 was delayed for several years by members of the [Seneca people](/wiki/Seneca_people "Seneca people"), who objected to the freeway's proposed routing through the [Allegany Indian Reservation](/wiki/Allegany_Indian_Reservation "Allegany Indian Reservation"). On June 29, 1976, the state of New York made an agreement with the Seneca nation that paid approximately $1.8 million (equivalent to $7.7 million in 2024[[22]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-22)) to the Seneca people and property owners for the 795 acres (322 ha) of land comprising the highway's proposed routing. In addition, the state ceded 795 acres (322 ha) of land to the Seneca people—750 (300) of which were taken from the adjacent [Allegany State Park](/wiki/Allegany_State_Park "Allegany State Park")—and agreed to support several tax and regulatory exemptions for the Senecas.[[23]](#cite_note-inflation-US-23)[[31]](#cite_note-31) The transaction was completed in September 1981,[[32]](#cite_note-32) and construction on the segment began in 1982.[[33]](#cite_note-sg-1985-33) The portion of the expressway between exits 20 and 21 was completed by 1985.[[28]](#cite_note-1985map-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/I-86_and_US_219_near_Salamanca%2C_NY.jpg/250px-I-86_and_US_219_near_Salamanca%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:I-86_and_US_219_near_Salamanca,_NY.jpg)

Eastbound on I-86 and southbound on US 219 near Salamanca

On July 21, 1985, construction was halted by protesting Senecas who did not accept the authority of the Seneca people’s leadership. The protest was organized in part by two owners of property in the path of the highway and involved the construction of an encampment on the right-of-way of the Southern Tier Expressway. The state had conducted studies on realigning the highway to bypass the disputed section;[[33]](#cite_note-sg-1985-33) however, the Indians vacated the encampment five days later. A temporary injunction prohibiting further disruptions of the highway's construction was issued in early August, allowing work on the Salamanca–Seneca Junction (exit 23) section of the expressway to resume on August 13.[[34]](#cite_note-34) This segment was completed by 1989[[29]](#cite_note-1989map-29) while the last section between Seneca Junction and Allegany was opened to traffic by 1995.[[35]](#cite_note-1995map-35)

Work on the Corning Bypass, a freeway around the northern and eastern fringes of the city of Corning, began in the mid-1980s. The first segment of the highway—between [NY 414](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_414 "New York State Route 414") (exit 46) and East Corning—was completed by 1989[[28]](#cite_note-1985map-28)[[29]](#cite_note-1989map-29) while the rest opened in the mid-1990s. The completion of the Corning Bypass, remedying the last substantial gap in the freeway, created a continuous, mostly limited-access highway between Erie, Pennsylvania, and Harriman, New York. The completed highway, designated as PA 17 and NY 17,[[35]](#cite_note-1995map-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) served as a time-saving, toll-free alternate route to the Thruway for motorists going from the [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") area to [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") and points west. In fact, the [New York State Thruway Authority](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway_Authority "New York State Thruway Authority") initially opposed the highway's construction, fearing the loss of toll revenue on its own route from motorists [shunpiking](/wiki/Shunpiking "Shunpiking") via the new highway.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Designation and conversion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Designation and conversion")]

The portion of the two-state freeway from I-90 near Erie to I-81 in Binghamton is designated as Corridor T of the [Appalachian Development Highway System](/wiki/Appalachian_Development_Highway_System "Appalachian Development Highway System").[[37]](#cite_note-adhs-ny-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) In 1998, all of PA 17 and the portion of NY 17 from the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") to Harriman were designated "High Priority Corridor 36" in the [Transportation Equity Act for the 21st Century](/wiki/Transportation_Equity_Act_for_the_21st_Century "Transportation Equity Act for the 21st Century") (TEA-21).[[39]](#cite_note-39) New York politicians, including Senator [Daniel Patrick Moynihan](/wiki/Daniel_Patrick_Moynihan "Daniel Patrick Moynihan"), and businesspeople backed the move in the hope that an efficient, high-speed roadway would inspire companies to start or expand their businesses in the state's southern counties.[[40]](#cite_note-ecdev-40) Shortly after the passage of TEA-21, Corridor 36 was legislatively designated as I-86 in an amendment to the bill.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The [American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials](/wiki/American_Association_of_State_Highway_and_Transportation_Officials "American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials") (AASHTO) formally approved the designation on November 6, 1998, as "Future I-86".[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/I-86_west_on_Horseheads_Bypass.jpg/250px-I-86_west_on_Horseheads_Bypass.jpg)](/wiki/File:I-86_west_on_Horseheads_Bypass.jpg)

Approaching exit 53 on the westbound Horseheads Bypass (I-86)

On December 3, 1999, all of PA 17 and the westernmost 177 miles (285 km) of NY 17 were officially designated as I-86 by the [Federal Highway Administration](/wiki/Federal_Highway_Administration "Federal Highway Administration") (FHWA)[[1]](#cite_note-fhwa-eis-1)[[43]](#cite_note-dotmap-43) following improvements to bring the roadway up to [Interstate Highway standards](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_standards "Interstate Highway standards").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The designation was extended eight miles (13 km) eastward to [NY 14](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14 "New York State Route 14") in [Horseheads](/wiki/Horseheads_(village),_New_York "Horseheads (village), New York") on January 28, 2004,[[43]](#cite_note-dotmap-43) after that section had been upgraded.[[44]](#cite_note-44) On May 1, 2006, the 10-mile (16 km) portion of NY 17 from I-81 in Binghamton east to [NY 79](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_79 "New York State Route 79") in [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_(village),_New_York "Windsor (village), New York") was designated as part of I-86[[43]](#cite_note-dotmap-43) following the elimination of [at-grade intersections](/wiki/At-grade_intersection "At-grade intersection") and the reconstruction of exit ramps along the stretch. The completion of the $30-million (equivalent to $44.8 million in 2024[[22]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-22)) project increased the total mileage of I-86 to 195 miles (314 km)[[45]](#cite_note-45) and created a temporary gap in the designation.[[43]](#cite_note-dotmap-43)

In Horseheads, a $60-million (equivalent to $87.2 million in 2024[[22]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-22)) project to elevate the highway and remove at-grade intersections in the village between NY 14 (exit 52) and [NY 13](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_13 "New York State Route 13") (exit 54) began in April 2004 and was completed on August 20, 2007. [NYSDOT](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") subsequently sought permission from the FHWA to extend I-86 over the new bypass and the existing NY 17 freeway to [NY 352](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_352 "New York State Route 352") in Elmira;[[46]](#cite_note-46) it was granted on March 28, 2008, adding another 5.9 miles (9.5 km) to the route.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

A 6.5-mile (10.5 km) portion of NY 17 between exits 56 and 59 originally had several at-grade intersections. Work on a project to eliminate the junctions began in January 2010.[[48]](#cite_note-2010jan-48) Three discontinuous sections of [County Route 60](/wiki/County_Route_60_(Chemung_County,_New_York) "County Route 60 (Chemung County, New York)") (CR 60, named Brant Road, Oneida Road, and Old NY 17), a parallel surface road, were linked together as part of the project.[[37]](#cite_note-adhs-ny-37) Two of the three at-grade junctions with CR 60—the east junction with Brant Road and the west junction with Oneida Road—were permanently closed on March 24, 2010, to allow construction to begin on the new alignment of the county route between the two locations.[[49]](#cite_note-49) The $65-million (equivalent to $87.5 million in 2024[[22]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-22)) project was completed on November 1, 2012.[[48]](#cite_note-2010jan-48)

On November 14, 2024, NYSDOT announced the extension of the I-86 designation from exit 60 to exit 67 ([NY 26](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_26 "New York State Route 26")) in [Vestal](/wiki/Vestal,_New_York "Vestal, New York").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

## Future

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Future")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/NY17_Future_I-86.jpg/250px-NY17_Future_I-86.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY17_Future_I-86.jpg)

A sign indicating NY 17's transition to I-86 near Goshen

In 1998, Governor [George Pataki](/wiki/George_Pataki "George Pataki") signed legislation to convert the entirety of [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17") to an interstate and stated that the conversion would be fully completed by 2009.[[51]](#cite_note-conversion2019-51) However, a severe lack of funding has pushed the completion date back. As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York)&action=edit), the only portion west of Binghamton not officially designated as I-86 is between the [Chemung](/wiki/Chemung_County,_New_York "Chemung County, New York")–[Tioga](/wiki/Tioga_County,_New_York "Tioga County, New York") county line and the junction with [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81 "Interstate 81"). The designation on this segment cannot be applied before NYSDOT completes the Prospect Mountain construction project at the junction of I-81, [US 11](/wiki/U.S._Route_11 "U.S. Route 11"), NY 17, and [NY 7](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_7 "New York State Route 7") in Binghamton,[[52]](#cite_note-52) which when complete will bring the roadway up to [Interstate Highway standards](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_standards "Interstate Highway standards"). The official completion of the project was set for December 2020.[[53]](#cite_note-53) The project has since been completed, but as of February 2022, the segment is still not officially designated as I-86.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Work on converting the portion of the highway east of Windsor is expected to be far more substantial than the work west of Binghamton.[[51]](#cite_note-conversion2019-51) Aside from numerous minor interchange improvements, major work includes constructing two new interchanges in the mountainous [Hale Eddy](/wiki/Hale_Eddy,_New_York "Hale Eddy, New York") area, exits 85 and 86, to replace two at-grade intersections, as well as the relocation of driveways in that area, improving curve radiuses throughout the route, and widening the shoulders on narrow parts of the highway. Work was completed in November 2019 on a redesigned interchange at exit 131, where NY 17 meets I-87 and [NY 32](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_32 "New York State Route 32").[[55]](#cite_note-55) Construction to bring exits 124 and 125 in [Goshen](/wiki/Goshen,_New_York "Goshen, New York") up to Interstate standards, with the latter being a brand new exit, was expected to be completed in early 2020, but was delayed.[[56]](#cite_note-56) In December 2020, NYSDOT completed construction of the new exit 125, which was built to accommodate the new [Legoland New York](/wiki/Legoland_New_York "Legoland New York"). As part of the project, a [four-ramp parclo](/wiki/Partial_cloverleaf_interchange "Partial cloverleaf interchange") was built, which replaced the prior exit 125, located 4,000 feet (1.2 km) west. NY 17 was also expanded to three lanes in each direction between exits 124 and 125. Harriman Drive was expanded to two lanes in each direction between the exit and Legoland's entrance as well.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) Exit 122 has also been upgraded to interstate standards.[[59]](#cite_note-59) There is no timetable for the full completion of the I-86 conversion between NY 79 in Windsor and the thruway (I-87) in Harriman. Nevertheless, the segment between [Bloomingburg](/wiki/Bloomingburg,_New_York "Bloomingburg, New York") and Goshen is signed as I-86 and NY 17 despite not officially being part of I-86.

In October 2022, Governor [Kathy Hochul](/wiki/Kathy_Hochul "Kathy Hochul") announced that a draft environmental impact statement on upgrading Route 17 to transform it into Interstate 86 was underway, public outreach was expected early in 2023, and that up to $1 billion was available for the work.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)
